title: Rosemary and peppermint cold-process soap
subtitle: A cooling, fresh-scented bar with rosemary and peppermint essential oils
excerpt: A cold-process bar soap with a rosemary and peppermint essential oil blend at 3.5% of oil weight. The oil base is 55% olive, 30% coconut, 15% castor for a stable, conditioning lather. Allow 4-6 weeks to harden before use.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997): essential oil behaviour in cold-process soap. SoapCalc (accessed May 2026): SAP values for this oil blend. EU Cosmetics Regulation 1223/2009 Annex III: peppermint (Mentha piperita) leaf oil safe use levels.

content.text: Rosemary essential oil is one of the tougher oils in cold-process soap. Its scent holds through the alkaline cure better than most citrus or light floral oils. Peppermint fades faster but gives an early lift and a soft cooling note in the lather. Together they make a clean herbal bar that reads as plain soap, not perfume.
Peppermint essential oil can accelerate
trace
noticeably in some blends. Mix the two essential oils together first in a small dish, then add both at once to the batter at light trace. Work quickly and pour the moment they are incorporated.

Peppermint essential oil is toxic to cats and dogs. Keep the soap away from household pets during the making session and label finished bars clearly. Wear protective gloves and goggles for all lye work. Add sodium hydroxide to water in a ventilated space.
What you need

items.name: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH)
items.prepNote: At 5% superfat for this blend. Weigh to 0.1 g.

items.name: Rosemary essential oil
items.prepNote: 2.4% of oil weight. Rosmarinus officinalis. Good tenacity in cold-process soap.

items.name: Peppermint essential oil
items.prepNote: 1% of oil weight. Mentha piperita. Keep below 1.5% to avoid skin sensitivity. Toxic to cats and dogs.

content.text: Method
Prepare the essential oil blend
Measure the rosemary and peppermint essential oils into a small dish and mix together. Having the blend ready to add immediately at trace avoids delays while the batter thickens.
Make the lye solution and prepare the oils

Gloves and goggles on. Add sodium hydroxide to the distilled water slowly and stir until dissolved. Allow to cool to 38-42°C. Melt the coconut oil and combine with the olive and castor oils. Allow to reach 38-42°C.
Blend to trace and mould
Pour the lye solution into the oils in a slow, steady stream. Stick-blend in 5-second bursts to reach light
trace
. Add the pre-mixed essential oil blend and stir in by hand or with a brief 5-second blast of the stick blender. Pour into the mould straight away. Insulate and leave 24-48 hours.
Unmould and cure
Wear gloves to unmould and cut. Cure on an open rack for 4-6 weeks. The
saponification
is complete well before the cure end; the curing time allows water to evaporate and the
superfat
oils to settle into the bar. The finished bar has a noticeably firm, smooth cut face and a clear green-herbal scent.
Storage and shelf life

Keep bars on an open rack in a cool, dark place. The bar keeps 2 years from cure date. The peppermint scent fades after 12 months without indicating spoilage. Discard on DOS (orange spots on the cut face) or a rancid paint-like smell. Label finished bars with the peppermint content if gifting to households with cats or dogs.
